Curriculum → Foundations and estimation → Back-of-the-envelope estimation

Lesson 2 of 5

Back-of-the-envelope estimation

Use quick arithmetic to size read/write traffic, storage growth, cache footprint, and inter-service bandwidth before deep diving.

25-35 min Foundations and estimation 4 guided stops

Study map

What this lesson helps you explain

Interviewers care less about perfect numbers than about whether your estimates are directionally correct and connected to design decisions.

Start here Frame the trade-off before diving into architecture. 1. Estimate the steady state Peak QPS is often 2x to 10x the average depending on workload shape2. Use mental reference numbers 1 KB ≈ 10^3 bytes, 1 MB ≈ 10^6 bytes, 1 GB ≈ 10^9 bytes3. Tie estimates to architecture choices Estimate hot-key risk, not just total volume

Guided walkthrough

Move through the lesson in a clear interview order

4 steps

Start with the framing, walk the concept in order, then use the right rail for prompts, pitfalls, and visual anchors.

1. Interview framing Interviewers care less about perfect numbers than about whether your estimates are directionally correct and connected to design decisions. 2. Estimate the steady state Peak QPS is often 2x to 10x the average depending on workload shape3. Use mental reference numbers 1 KB ≈ 10^3 bytes, 1 MB ≈ 10^6 bytes, 1 GB ≈ 10^9 bytes4. Tie estimates to architecture choices Estimate hot-key risk, not just total volume
1

Start with this framing

Interview framing

Interviewers care less about perfect numbers than about whether your estimates are directionally correct and connected to design decisions.

What to say first

  • Calculate average and peak QPS.
  • Estimate storage growth for 1 day and 1 year.
  • Account for replicas, indexes, and cached copies.
2

Study section

Estimate the steady state

Start from DAU, requests per user, and object size. Convert the product story into QPS, storage growth per day, and bandwidth per request path.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Peak QPS is often 2x to 10x the average depending on workload shape
  • Write amplification matters for indexes, replicas, and event logs
  • Storage growth should include primary data, indexes, backups, and derived views
3

Study section

Use mental reference numbers

Latency numbers, powers of two, and rough infrastructure limits help you reject unrealistic designs early.

Call these points out explicitly

  • 1 KB ≈ 10^3 bytes, 1 MB ≈ 10^6 bytes, 1 GB ≈ 10^9 bytes
  • Cross-region round trips are orders of magnitude slower than memory access
  • A cache hit that avoids disk or network hops usually changes the bottleneck dramatically
4

Study section

Tie estimates to architecture choices

Each estimate should justify something: a CDN for global static traffic, sharding for write throughput, or asynchronous pipelines for burst smoothing.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Estimate hot-key risk, not just total volume
  • Consider recovery bandwidth for backups and replica catch-up
  • Show how growth changes the next architectural step

Checklist

Things to say clearly

  • Calculate average and peak QPS.
  • Estimate storage growth for 1 day and 1 year.
  • Account for replicas, indexes, and cached copies.
  • Use numbers to justify scaling decisions rather than quoting them in isolation.

Common pitfalls

What to avoid

  • Forgetting that reads and writes fan out into caches, indexes, and logs.
  • Using unrealistic object sizes or ignoring metadata overhead.
  • Treating averages as enough for bursty traffic.

Interview prompts

Practice aloud

  • For a URL shortener with 100 million new links per month, what is the yearly storage requirement?
  • How would a read-heavy social feed change your cache estimate?
  • When is a rough estimate sufficient and when should you refine it?

Related topics

Keep the thread going

Problem framing and requirements Foundations and estimationCaching layers and cache placement Caching, asynchrony, and resilience

Simulation lab

Simulation lab: Back-of-the-envelope estimation

Turn estimation and baseline trade-offs into an actual workload so you can see which assumption breaks first. Apply it to back-of-the-envelope estimation so the trade-offs feel concrete.

Use this lab to turn "Back-of-the-envelope estimation" into a measurable system instead of a static definition.Pressure the path around estimate the steady state and watch how it interacts with use mental reference numbers.Interviewers care less about perfect numbers than about whether your estimates are directionally correct and connected to design decisions.

Choose an API path

Which flow are you stressing?

Focus metrics

  • p95 latency
  • cache hit ratio
  • replica utilization
  • retry amplification

Workload profile

Traffic shape

Exercise workspace

Multi-file topology and override editor

Use file tabs, code completion, inline previews, and diagnostics to tune the topology and scripted overrides without leaving the browser.

topology.flow Edit the topology and override script with quick inserts, inline diagnostics, and visible structure signals.

Signals

What the editor found

  • Line 1: edge · 90000 rps · 4 ms
  • Line 2: service · 48000 rps · 8 ms
  • Line 3: service · 26000 rps · 14 ms
  • Line 4: cache · 110000 rps · 3 ms
  • Line 5: database · 9000 rps · 22 ms
  • Line 6: database · 18000 rps · 16 ms

Built-in runtime adapters

Browser and WebAssembly execution targets

No eval

The simulator runs its DSL interpreters in-browser today and now exposes adapter slots for WebAssembly-backed runtimes like Pyodide or custom interpreters.

Native flow-graph compiler

Runs the topology and simulation DSL interpreters directly in the browser.

Available now
Pyodide / Python WASM adapter

Ready for browser-side Python interpreters compiled to WebAssembly.

Register adapter
Lua / custom WASM adapter

Use this slot for additional in-browser interpreters backed by WebAssembly modules.

Register adapter

Good first experiments

  • Calculate average and peak QPS.
  • Estimate storage growth for 1 day and 1 year.
  • Account for replicas, indexes, and cached copies.

API modeling notes

How this simulator thinks

  • Start with the critical path from Estimate the steady state and use the metrics to explain where latency or capacity moves.
  • Use the simulator to justify checklist items like Calculate average and peak QPS. before you propose scaling changes.
  • Treat the async queue and worker path as the escape hatch for ideas from Use mental reference numbers.

What you get after a run

Simulation output

  • Per-node utilization, queue depth, and dropped traffic
  • Average, p95, and p99 latency for the selected API path
  • Probable fixes mapped to the dominant bottleneck

Interactive practice lab

Write, save, and move through the mock interview

Answers stay in your browser so you can come back later without needing automated grading.

0/3 prompts saved Prompt 1 of 3

Prompt flow

What you will practice

interview

Explain the concept

Not saved yet

Teach "Back-of-the-envelope estimation" back to an interviewer. Summarize what it is, when you would use it, and why it matters in a real system.

Goal for this step

Explain the topic in interviewer-friendly language before diving into implementation details.

Use these guardrails

  • For a URL shortener with 100 million new links per month, what is the yearly storage requirement?
  • Estimate the steady state
  • Use mental reference numbers
  • Calculate average and peak QPS.

Suggested structure

  • Definition
  • When to use it
  • Benefits
  • Trade-offs

Bring your own model
